Acquisition Target Risk:
INSIGHT: Ola Electric Is Vulnerable to Acquisition at Depressed Valuation
With a market cap of Rs 15,923 Cr (~$1.9B), stock at Rs 36.10 (76% below peak), and FIIs exiting (5.41% to 3.99%), Ola Electric is vulnerable to acquisition. A strategic buyer (Mahindra, Hero, international OEM) could acquire a controlling stake for $1.5-2B -- cheap relative to the Gigafactory asset value (20 GWh target capacity, built at significant investment). Pre-IPO investor lockup expiry (SoftBank, Tiger Global) creates selling pressure that aligns with an acquisition scenario, and the increasing retail concentration (54.39%) with declining institutional support creates a shareholder base with limited ability to resist a premium offer.

Acquisition Target Risk

Ola Electric's dramatic stock price collapse -- from an IPO peak of approximately Rs 150 to a current Rs 36.10, a 76% decline -- has reduced the company's market capitalisation to Rs 15,923 Cr (~$1.9 billion). At this valuation, Ola's Gigafactory assets alone (20 GWh target capacity, already at 6 GWh operational, built at significant capital investment) may be worth more to a strategic buyer than the current market capitalisation suggests, creating a classic asset-stripping or consolidation opportunity.

The shareholder structure amplifies this vulnerability. Promoter Bhavish Aggarwal holds 34.59%, meaning a bidder needs only ~15.5% more to gain effective control if the promoter does not participate. Pre-IPO investors (SoftBank Vision Fund with 21.98% pre-IPO stake at Rs 50.94/share weighted average cost, Tiger Global, and others) face lockup expiry and are likely sellers given current losses on their investment. FIIs have been consistently reducing (from 5.41% to 3.99% in 9 months), while retail investors -- who now hold 54.39% -- are typically less able to evaluate tender offers or resist a premium bid.

Potential strategic acquirers include: Mahindra & Mahindra (aspiring EV player without a dedicated 2W EV platform), Hero MotoCorp (would gain Gigafactory capacity and eliminate a competitor), or an international OEM seeking India market entry (BYD, Honda, or a European player without 2W presence in India). The discounted valuation, Gigafactory asset base, and depressed stock price create an attractive entry point that may not persist once Ola's recovery story gains credibility.

D2C Model Fixed Cost Trap:
INSIGHT: Ola's D2C Model Created a Fixed Cost Structure That Became Unsustainable When Volumes Collapsed
Ola's direct-to-consumer model required expensive company-owned experience centres, service infrastructure, and a large sales workforce -- designed for 50%+ market share but catastrophically overbuilt for 16%. Selling & admin expenses ballooned from Rs 58 Cr (FY21) to Rs 1,598 Cr (FY25) -- a 27.5x increase -- and reached 35.4% of revenue by FY25. When the service crisis destroyed volumes, these fixed costs could not be reduced proportionally. Only after extreme OpEx cuts (Rs 840 Cr to Rs 484 Cr, a 42% reduction in two quarters) did the cost structure become survivable, with the breakeven threshold lowered from 40,000 to 15,000 units/month.

D2C Model Fixed Cost Trap

Ola Electric's direct-to-consumer (D2C) model was initially hailed as a disruptive innovation that eliminated dealer margins and gave Ola direct customer ownership. In practice, it created a fixed cost structure that became the company's single largest financial vulnerability. Unlike legacy OEMs whose dealer networks operate on variable cost models (dealers bear real estate, staffing, and inventory costs), Ola's company-owned experience centres placed every rupee of retail infrastructure cost -- rent, salaries, utilities, training -- onto Ola's own P&L.

The scale of this fixed cost commitment was enormous. At its peak (Sep 2023), Ola operated 935 experience centres and 414 service centres -- "India's largest company-owned network" according to the DRHP -- managed initially through the related party Ola Fleet Technologies and then brought in-house in Jul 2023. The SG&A line grew from Rs 58 Cr (FY21, pre-commercial) to Rs 1,598 Cr (FY25), representing 35.4% of revenue. This cost structure was built for the 50%+ market share environment and became a financial straitjacket when volumes collapsed.

The trap mechanism is self-reinforcing. When the service crisis emerged, Ola needed more service capacity to fix trust issues, but the revenue decline made additional network investment unaffordable. Between Q4 FY25 and Q3 FY26, Ola slashed OpEx from Rs 840 Cr to Rs 484 Cr (42% reduction) -- firing sales staff, closing underperforming centres, and cutting marketing. This restored financial viability (breakeven lowered to 15,000 units/month) but reduced the service and sales capacity needed to drive volume recovery. The D2C model, designed as a competitive advantage, became an anchor that constrained Ola's ability to invest its way out of the crisis.

Key Data Points

  • SG&A: Rs 58 Cr (FY21) → Rs 1,598 Cr (FY25) -- 27.5x increase
  • SG&A as % of revenue: 35.4% in FY25 -- unsustainable for an automotive company
  • Peak experience centres: 935 (Sep 2023), only 300 with repair capability
  • Service centres: 414 at peak (410 co-located within experience centres)
  • OpEx cut: Rs 840 Cr (Q4 FY25 peak) → Rs 484 Cr (Q3 FY26) -- 42% reduction in two quarters
  • Breakeven threshold: reduced from 40,000+ to 15,000 units/month through cost cutting
  • Ola Fleet Technologies fee in FY23: Rs 722.6 Cr -- related party cost of the D2C network
  • Legacy OEM equivalent: Hero's 6,000+ dealers operate on variable-cost franchise model
  • By comparison, Bajaj's service cost is Rs 400/visit (2-4x cheaper) with TPM-certified quality

Ola Product Mix ASP Trap:
INSIGHT: Ola's Product Mix Shift to Low-End Models Destroyed ASP While Gross Margins Paradoxically Improved
Ola's product mix shifted dramatically from 35% premium S1 Pro (Q1 FY25) to 75% low-end S1 X (Q3 FY26), destroying blended ASP from Rs 135,000 to Rs 89,000 (-34%). This was a defensive move to maintain volume as premium buyers fled due to service concerns. However, gross margins improved from 18% to 36% during the same period because the S1 X has simpler build economics and the Gigafactory reduced battery costs. The paradox: Ola makes more margin per unit on a cheaper scooter, but the bottom-line contribution is insufficient because total volumes are too low (35K vs peak 108K quarterly).

Ola Product Mix ASP Trap

Ola Electric's product mix has undergone a dramatic shift from premium-heavy to budget-heavy, creating what can be described as an ASP (Average Selling Price) trap. The S1 X -- Ola's cheapest model starting at Rs 69,999 -- grew from 15% of product mix (Q1 FY25) to 75% (Q3 FY26), while the premium S1 Pro collapsed from 35% to just 6%. Blended ASP fell from Rs 135,000 to Rs 89,000 in seven quarters -- a 34% decline.

This shift was not strategic but defensive. Premium customers (S1 Pro buyers) abandoned Ola due to the service crisis and trust erosion, leaving Ola with no choice but to sell more S1 X models to budget-conscious customers who were less informed about or less concerned by the service issues. The consequence was a self-reinforcing revenue decline: each quarter's mix shifted further toward the low end, bringing down the ASP and reducing per-unit revenue contribution, even as total costs could not be reduced proportionally.

Paradoxically, the same period saw Ola's gross margins improve from 18% (Q1 FY25) to 36% (Q4 FY26). This was driven by two factors: the S1 X has a simpler build with fewer components and lower BOM cost, and in-house NMC 4680 cell production (Gigafactory) reduced battery cost from $130/kWh to $85/kWh. The result is that Ola now makes higher margins on a cheaper product -- but the absolute rupee contribution per unit is still lower because the revenue base has shrunk, and total volumes at 35,000-39,000 units/quarter are far below the 108,000-unit peak.

The trap becomes apparent when considering the recovery path. To grow revenue, Ola must either (a) sell significantly more S1 X units, which requires regaining the trust of budget buyers, or (b) shift the mix back toward premium models, which requires convincing premium buyers that the service crisis is resolved -- a taller order given the trust damage at that segment. Neither path is easy, and both require time that Ola's cash runway (12-18 months) may not provide.

Key Data Points

  • S1 Pro share of mix: 35% (Q1 FY25) → 6% (Q3 FY26) -- premium segment effectively exited
  • S1 X share of mix: 15% (Q1 FY25) → 75% (Q3 FY26) -- budget segment dominance
  • Blended ASP: Rs 135,000 (Q1 FY25) → Rs 89,000 (Q4 FY26) -- 34% decline
  • Gross margin: 18% (Q1 FY25) → 36% (Q4 FY26) -- 2x improvement despite ASP decline
  • Quarterly units: 108,000 (peak, Q1 FY25) → 35,000-39,000 (Q3-Q4 FY26) -- 64-68% decline
  • Battery cost: $130/kWh (imported, Q1 FY25) → $85/kWh (in-house, Q4 FY26) -- 35% reduction
  • Ola blended FY26 revenue per unit: Rs 223,902 (misleadingly high due to partial-year mix distortion)
  • Competitive comparison: Ather maintains consistent Rs 139,582/unit ASP without extreme discounting
  • Ola's S1 X (3kWh) at Rs 89,999 directly competes with Bajaj Chetak Base at Rs 91,000 -- price parity but trust deficit

Supply Chain:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing
Ola's supply chain risk is fundamentally a battery cost and availability dependency — the battery represents 35% of BOM for the Ola S1 Pro (single largest component), and cells were imported without contracts exceeding 1 year. The Technology/Gigafactory_Updates was the strategic fix, but it created a new set of risks: only ~16% capacity utilization (6 GWh built, ~1 GWh consumed), cell cost at $80/kWh (import parity, not advantage), and a B2B pivot with zero confirmed orders as of May 2026. The global context is brutal: 4,200 GWh global cell capacity, ~900 GWh excess — Ola is trying to compete in a market flooded with cheap cells.

Gigafactory Execution:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing
The Technology/Gigafactory_Updates is Ola's highest-stakes strategic initiative — a Rs 4,000+ Cr bet on domestic cell manufacturing that faces three existential risks simultaneously: (1) catastrophic under-utilization — 6.0 GWh capacity at only ~16% utilization (~1 GWh consumed vs 6 GWh built), (2) import parity, not advantage — Ola's $80/kWh LFP cost only matches (doesn't beat) the global LFP price of $80/kWh, and (3) a B2B pivot with ZERO confirmed orders — the stated hedge against under-utilization has no revenue evidence as of May 2026. Without execution, Rs 4,000+ Cr of capex becomes a stranded asset.

Gigafactory Execution Risk

Criticality: 🔴 HIGH

The Stakes

Ola Gigafactory is the single most important strategic initiative for Companies/Ola Electric. Success means cost leadership and supply independence. Failure means continued import dependence, subsidy penalties, and competitive disadvantage.

Financial Risk

  • OCT must meet minimum investment + employment obligations per SIPCOT MoU
  • Offtake agreement: OET must purchase cells from OCT (6-year agreement, minimum commitments)
  • Default triggers: nominee director appointment, debt-to-equity conversion, security enforcement
  • Stranded asset risk: 6 GWh built at ~Rs 4,000 Cr capex, only ~1 GWh consumed — 90%+ idle capacity

Competitive Risk

  • Cell technology evolving rapidly — LFP, Technology/Cobalt Supply Risk and LFP Pivot, and new chemistries competing
  • Global overcapacity: 4,200 GWh global capacity with 900 GWh excess — "bad if selling B2B"
  • Risk of obsolescence if LFP 46100 doesn't achieve cost/performance targets vs next-gen chemistries (solid state, sodium-ion)
  • Chinese manufacturers (CATL, BYD) have massive scale at $80/kWh — Ola has zero cost advantage

Revenue Decline FY25:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing
Ola's revenue decline of 9.9% in FY25 (Rs 5,010 Cr → Rs 4,514 Cr) masks a far deeper collapse: quarterly revenue fell 71% from peak (Rs 1,644 Cr in Q1 FY25) to trough (Rs 470 Cr in Q4 FY26) — then partially recovered to Rs 520 Cr. The full-year decline was cushioned by H1 FY25 being strong before the Risks/Service_Center_Crisis fully hit. But the underlying trend is brutal: monthly sales dropped from 53,320 units (Mar-24) to 7,567 (Nov-25) — an 85.8% decline. The decline is a direct consequence of the trust crisis, not market contraction — the total E2W market grew from 119K to 149K monthly units over the same period.

Revenue Decline FY25

Criticality: 🔴 HIGH

---

The Reversal

After explosive growth from FY22-FY24 (Rs 373 Cr → Rs 5,010 Cr), Companies/Ola Electric's revenue declined 9.9% in FY25 to Rs 4,514 Cr. The quarterly decline was far steeper.

---

What Changed

External

  • FAME subsidy reduction (40%→15% cap) increased vehicle prices by Rs 22,784-Rs 37,106
  • Competition intensified as incumbents scaled EV offerings — Companies/TVS Motor reached 23% share, Companies/Bajaj Auto hit 21%, Companies/Ather Energy grew 75% YoY
  • Market/PM E-DRIVE Subsidy subsidy ends Jul 2026 — creating additional price risk for H2 FY27

Internal

  • Quality concerns accumulated, affecting repeat purchases and word-of-mouth
  • Service gaps created friction in customer experience
  • Brand Perception shifted from "Exciting" to "Unreliable"
  • The Risks/CCPA_Regulatory_Intervention show-cause notice (Oct 2024) triggered negative media coverage across ET, Mint, Business Standard

Implications

1. Cash burn accelerates — lower revenue + fixed costs = larger losses (FY25 loss: Rs 2,276 Cr, worst ever)
2. Market share loss — revenue declining while market growing: went from 44.69% to 15.95% share
3. Financing dependence — FY25 financing cash flow +Rs 5,429 Cr to stay afloat; cash at Rs 3,516 Cr (Mar-25)
4. Valuation erosion — stock at Rs 36.1 (80.7% below post-IPO peak ~Rs 150), market cap Rs 15,923 Cr
5. Financial/Margin_Paradox — despite revenue decline, the company is restructuring into viability at lower volumes

Ola received a CCPA show-cause notice in October 2024 after consumer complaints exceeded 10,000 — making it one of the Top 5 most complained-about brands across ALL industries on India's National Consumer Helpline. Consumer court cases escalated from 189 (pre-IPO disclosure) to 500+ by May 2026, with courts ordering full refunds plus Rs 10,000-50,000 compensation citing a "pattern of neglect." The regulatory crisis was the catalytic event that broke consumer inertia and triggered Ola's market share collapse from 26.4% (Oct-24) to 8.6% (Oct-25).

The CCPA Show-Cause Notice

The Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA) is empowered to investigate "unfair trade practices" and "consumer rights violations." The 10,000+ complaints — unprecedented for an EV company — crossed the regulatory threshold requiring formal action.

Why this was devastating:
1. Government validation — the CCPA notice officially confirmed what individual complaints could not: systematic consumer harm
2. Media amplification — ET, Mint, and Business Standard covered it prominently, transforming "customer grumbles" into "regulatory crisis"
3. Two-front pressure — MHI simultaneously asked ARAI to investigate subsidy eligibility, suggesting regulatory alignment against Ola
4. Consumer behavior break — Nov-24 VAHAN data showed TVS overtaking Ola for the first time, the direct causal lag

Consumer Court Crisis:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing
Consumer court cases against Ola Electric grew from 189 (disclosed in DRHP, Aug-2024) to an estimated 500+ by May-2026 — a 164% post-IPO escalation. Multiple district consumer forums have ruled against Ola, ordering full refunds plus Rs 10,000-50,000 compensation per case, with courts citing a "pattern of neglect." By Q4 FY25, reports emerged of bailable warrants being issued over unresolved consumer court summons, transforming the crisis from a customer relations problem into a personal legal liability for management. Each adverse ruling sustains negative media coverage that prevents market share recovery.

Consumer Court Crisis

The consumer court crisis represents the legal dimension of Ola Electric's service failure, distinct from the regulatory (CCPA) and operational (service center) crises but deeply interconnected with both. While the CCPA show-cause notice (Oct 2024) was the public regulatory inflection point, the consumer court cases represent individual consumer remedies that collectively paint a devastating picture of Ola's customer commitment. The number of cases escalated from 189 at IPO (Aug 2024) to 500+ by May 2026, suggesting that the DRHP disclosure significantly understated the trajectory of consumer litigation.

Legal Findings and Patterns

The most damaging aspect of the consumer court rulings is not the financial compensation (Rs 10,000-50,000 per case, totaling an estimated Rs 5-10 Cr liability at 500+ cases) but the judicial findings. Courts have consistently cited a "pattern of neglect" — language that moves beyond individual grievance to systemic failure. This judicial characterization has several implications:

First, it creates a public legal record that any prospective buyer can discover. Each ruling is independently reported by local media, generating recurring negative news cycles that reinforce the "unreliable brand" narrative. Second, the "pattern of neglect" finding could be used by the CCPA in its ongoing investigation as evidence of systematic consumer harm, potentially triggering heavier penalties or operational restrictions. Third, it emboldens more customers to file cases — the existence of favorable precedents reduces the friction of legal action, potentially creating a self-reinforcing litigation spiral.

Escalation to Personal Legal Risk

The most alarming development was the emergence of reports in Q4 FY25 that consumer courts had issued bailable warrants over unresolved summons against Ola Electric management. This escalation signals that the company was failing to respond to court notices — a level of organizational dysfunction that transformed a civil consumer matter into a criminal liability concern. Bailable warrants mean that named respondents (potentially including founder Bhavish Aggarwal and senior management) could face arrest if they fail to appear before the court, creating personal legal risk that distracts management from the operational turnaround.

The escalation to warrants suggests Ola's legal response infrastructure was overwhelmed — the same pattern as its service infrastructure. Just as the company had ~300 repair-capable centers for 1M+ vehicles, it may have had an inadequate legal team to respond to 500+ simultaneous consumer cases across India's district forum system. Each missed court date compounds the legal jeopardy and signals to the judiciary that the company is treating consumer grievances with contempt.

Financial and Reputational Liability

The direct financial liability from consumer court orders remains modest relative to Ola's scale — an estimated Rs 5-10 Cr in total compensation at 500+ cases, compared to operating expenses of Rs 741 Cr/quarter. However, the indirect costs are substantial. Each ruling requires Ola to refund the purchase price (Rs 70,000-1,99,000) plus compensation, meaning the effective cost per lost case is approximately Rs 1-1.5 lakh. More importantly, the adverse orders create a feedback loop with the service crisis: customers who receive refunds through court orders become vocal brand detractors, and their stories amplify the negative NPS (below 0) that already characterizes Ola's brand perception.

The litigation also creates uncertainty for Ola's IPO investors and potential acquirers. The 164% post-IPO case escalation (189 to 500+) suggests the problem is growing, not stabilizing. For a potential strategic acquirer evaluating Ola at its depressed Rs 15,923 Cr market cap, the unresolved consumer litigation represents contingent liability that would require provisioning. For FIIs (who reduced holdings from 5.41% to 3.99% over four quarters), the legal overhang reinforces the bear case.

Key Data Points

  • Pre-IPO consumer court cases disclosed in DRHP (Aug 2024): 189
  • Post-IPO estimated cases (May 2026): 500+ — 164% increase in 18 months
  • Typical court ruling: Full refund + Rs 10,000-50,000 compensation
  • Judicial finding: "Pattern of neglect" — systemic, not isolated failures
  • Bailable warrants reported: Q4 FY25 — management personal legal risk
  • Some cases escalated to High Court level
  • Estimated total compensation liability at 500+ cases: Rs 5-10 Cr
  • Effective cost per lost case (refund + compensation): ~Rs 1-1.5 lakh
  • CCPA complaint volume: 10,000+ — Top 5 among all Indian companies across all sectors
  • FII exodus: 5.41% to 3.99% over four consecutive quarters
  • Stock decline: Rs 150 peak to Rs 36.10 (-76%) — legal overhang contributes to bearish sentiment

Subsidy Dependency:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing
India's E2W subsidy regime is on a one-way path from generous (FAME-II: Rs 40,000/vehicle) to terminal (Jul 2026: Rs 0/vehicle). Each policy inflection directly maps to Ola's financial outcomes: the Mar-2024 FAME-II expiry triggered a 36% single-month volume collapse and a 92.5% passthrough of subsidy loss to ASP cuts (Rs 1,35,000 → Rs 98,000), crushing EBITDA to -22.1% within 3 quarters. The current PM E-DRIVE subsidy of Rs 5,000 represents only 5.6% of ASP — but its removal would consume 123% of current per-unit profit (Rs 4,050).

INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing
Ola's service center crisis is the single largest driver of its market share collapse from 55.81% (Apr-2022) to 8.6% (Oct-2025) — an 80.8% relative decline. The D2C model with only ~300 repair-capable centers (vs TVS Motor's 1,000+ and Bajaj Auto's 1,480+) created 7-15 day repair backlogs that triggered a compounding death spiral: complaints → CCPA intervention → negative media → buyer deterrence → sales collapse. Consumer Court Patterns

  • District Consumer Forums across India have ruled against Ola multiple times
  • Common orders: Full refund + compensation for "mental agony" + litigation costs
  • Courts have cited "pattern of neglect" — systemic, not isolated incidents
  • Some cases escalated to High Court level
  • Reports of bailable warrants issued over unresolved consumer court summons

The Root Cause: D2C Model Failure at Scale

Ola's D2C (Direct-to-Consumer) service model worked at low volumes but catastrophically failed at scale:

| Problem | Detail |
|---------|--------|
| No independent mechanics | Unlike Companies/TVS Motor/Companies/Bajaj Auto where any roadside mechanic can service an ICE scooter, Ola scooters REQUIRE company-trained technicians |
| Spare parts bottleneck | Parts were centrally managed — no distributor network like Legacy OEMs |
| Software dependency | Many issues required OTA updates or app-based diagnostics — couldn't be fixed physically |
| Experience centers ≠ service centers | Ola's 900 "experience centers" were designed for sales, not repair. Limited service bays and tools |
| Geographic gaps | Customers in Tier 2/3 cities had no service access at all |

Why Legacy Networks Are a "Moat"

Legacy OEMs don't just have more service centers — they have a fundamentally different service architecture:

1. Decades of mechanic training: Companies/TVS Motor and Companies/Bajaj Auto have trained hundreds of thousands of mechanics over 30+ years. These mechanics already know how to handle electric variants with minimal retraining.

2. Distributed parts inventory: Legacy OEMs have regional warehouses, state distributors, and local dealers who stock parts. When a Chetak needs a brake pad, it's available in the same town. When an Ola S1 needs a battery module, it ships from Market/State_EV_Policies.

3. Trust inheritance: A customer who has owned 3 TVS Jupiter scooters already trusts TVS. Buying an iQube is an easy decision because TVS's service promise carries over. Ola has NO trust inheritance.

4. Cost structure: Companies/Bajaj Auto's Chetak service costs ₹400 per 5,000 km. That's comparable to ICE scooters. Ola's service model is unpredictable in both cost and timeline.

Part 3: The Hyperservice Response

What Ola Did (Q3 FY25 → Q3 FY26)

| Initiative | Detail | Impact | |-----------|--------|--------| | Project Vistaar (Nov 2024) | Network transformation — optimize retail + service footprint | Reduced delivery time from 12 days to 3-4 days | | HyperDelivery | Same-day delivery capability at select centers | Improved customer acquisition at point-of-sale | | Hyperservice Vans | 1,000 mobile service vans deployed to reach customers | Extends service reach beyond fixed centers | | AI Diagnostics | Remote troubleshooting via app, automated issue detection | 80% of service tickets resolved same-day (Ola claim) | | Project Lakshya | Cost optimization — OpEx target ₹250-300 Cr/quarter | Breakeven lowered to 15,000 units/month | | Service Backlog Clearance | Dedicated sprint to clear accumulated service tickets | 50% reduction in backlog (Ola claim) |

Assessment: Is it Working?

Partially. The operational metrics have improved, but the brand damage is done:
  • Operationally: Service response times have improved significantly
  • Financially: The leaner model works (34.3% gross margin proves it)
  • Commercially: Sales have NOT recovered. Monthly volumes in Q3/Q4 FY26 averaged ~12,000 units — far below the 15,000 breakeven

The core issue: Trust, once lost, takes 2-3 product cycles to rebuild. Companies/TVS Motor and Companies/Bajaj Auto customers who switched during the crisis will not switch back for at least 2-3 years.

Connections

### Causes

- D2C service model designed for low volumes, inadequate for scale

- Only ~300 of 900 experience centers had repair capability

- Centralized spare parts management created distribution bottlenecks

- No trust inheritance — Ola had zero brand equity in automotive service

- Hypergrowth (12,000 to 53,320 monthly registrations in 23 months) overwhelmed infrastructure

### Effects

- Market share collapse: 55.81% → 8.6% (18 months)

- Revenue decline: -71% peak-to-trough (Rs 1,644 Cr → Rs 450 Cr quarterly)

- Stock crash: ~Rs 150 post-IPO peak to ~Rs 29 (80.7% decline)

- Brand destruction: Market/Net_Promoter_Score negative (below 0) for 2025-2026

- Regulatory jeopardy: CCPA investigation + MHI/ARAI subsidy probe

- Competitive surrender: Companies/TVS Motor (23%), Companies/Bajaj Auto (21%), Companies/Ather Energy (16%), Hero (11%) captured lost share

### Amplifiers

- News media coverage (ET, Mint, BS) gave CCPA action front-page prominence

- Social media virality (#OlaElectric trending, Reddit recommending competitors)

- Consumer court rulings created public legal records deterring new buyers

- Negative NPS means each existing owner becomes a detractor influencing 10-20 potential buyers

- Google Play rating gap (3.2 vs TVS 4.2, Ather 4.0) visible at every purchase research touchpoint

WARNING: Critical Incident: The Pune Fire
On March 26, 2022, an Ola S1 Pro caught fire in Pune, Maharashtra. Ola ultimately paid a penalty of ₹1,500,000.
